Levvo: I think.. middlegame should be more about very basic concepts
Levvo: First off, you need a  thought process to know what you are looking for in the game.
Levvo: And I'm sure you know it alredyt.
Levvo: Are any of my groups weak / can get weak in one move, then fix it.
Levvo: Are any of my opponents groups weak / can get weak, then attack it.
Levvo: And urgent is all about weakness.
Levvo: And this is why you need to do alot of tsumego.
Levvo: Because you always need to know the state of your groups.
Levvo: ""Am I alive, or do I HAVE to defend""
Levvo: Also there is a ""I am alive but I should defend or he can use the group to his advantage""
Levvo: Then you need to value your options.
Levvo: ""What happens if he cuts me here?""
Levvo: ""Is a big move worth more then letting him invade""
Levvo: Or maybe defending the invasion is the big move.

Levvo: Generally I like to think of each group as their own unit. Then put a healthbar on it. And rank all your groups health in your head. ""This is critically unhealthy, This is so healthy I could't kill it with a gun .. etc"" :P
Levvo: For example, your biggest mistake in this game was to not protect your d15 group.
Levvo: So ofcourse just hearing ""Do tsumego"" wont help much. But if you consider it, its realy the only thing you need to do to get alot better.
Levvo: With the right mindset ofcourse.

Levvo: First you want to know which side is bigger
Levvo: A or B, and it's a smiple - which has more space.

Levvo: Then you need to look at the closest to either your answer move at B, or pincer move at A (Or any variation of the pincers / answers). You want to know what side to play on, that's the most importaint - and whatever move you play on that side doesn't really matter too much at your level.
Levvo: So first you count spaces - here A side is 7 spaces and B side is 5 spaces, so the A area is bigger.
Levvo: But that's not enough, you need to know what will happen afterwords and what will happen to the closest groups.
Levvo: If you play in an area where white is strong - you are likely to get attacked.
Levvo: If you play in an area where you are strong you might be overconcentrated.
Levvo: So as I see it whites F4 group  is quite strong. So if you pincer you need to know that F4 is strong already and O3 is about as strong as your pincer stone.
Levvo: Also you need to see if there is space enough to pincer. Here you have space for a base atleast so it seems possible to pincer if you want too.
Levvo: And if we think of F4 as not super strong you have to consider that if you play close to it it will get stronger- so what will happen to your groups in the area?
Levvo: In this case we look at your C5 group. Which is surrounded but - is it alive unconditionally?
Levvo: If it's not fully alive, then making F4 stronger can be a bad idea.

C[Wulfenia: I have thought some more about your explanations. I am not sure what you mean with ""corners are more value than sides"". Certainly, a corner is easier to settle and can extend to two sides, but shouldn't potential be incorporated in value everywhere? I understand why it is a good idea to play corners before playing some random move in the middle of the board. But I have always thought that the reason is not that a first move at 4-4 is intrinsically more valuable than a first move at, say 7-9, but that we simply do not really know how to use the potential of the 7-9 stone properly. archpaladin1: Move 15 I think was poor in retrospect.  I should have played around where 17 was or moved to strengthen something else.
archpaladin1: What is the best response to 18?  I figured I could treat G17 lightly and try and consolidate my stones along the top and upper right, but I don't think I did well in retrospect.
archpaladin1: Move 23 was also poor in retrospect because of white's follow-up at N16.  It was too early to tenuki, but I was afraid of one of my other groups being oppressed so early.
archpaladin1: Move 39 was a last-ditch effort to save the top.

archpaladin1: White's sequence from 40 to 44 is something I'd like to learn how to do better.  White makes shape while cutting through black.  I don't feel like I can do this without extensive time sitting to read and hope my opponent responds correctly.  Is there a good way to read your opponent's intention in his moves so that you know how to leverage that for your own gain?  Or is it just experience?

archpaladin1: Move 49 brings up an example of a habit I'd like to break.  Black should understand that there are much bigger moves to play.  But I'm used to plodding on and cutting through enough because my opponent typically either makes a mistake or loses fighting spirit.  When fighting with a much stronger player I can't rely on these assumptions, and I don't have a good method to replace them with yet.
